nodes_flat.title: Executive Director Welcome Message
nodes_flat.topics: School welcome; New families; Handbook purpose; School operations; Executive Director
nodes_flat.content:
Dear Families,

The Imagine Montessori School Valencia Team welcomes the entire school community to the 2024/25 school year, especially the families joining our community for the first time.

This handbook includes relevant information that will serve as a guide on the general operation of our school. We hope you find it useful.

Kind regards,

![Mujer mayor sentada en una mesaDescripción generada automáticamente con confianza baja][image1]

**Mariano Pontón**
**Imagine Montessori School’s Executive Director**

nodes_flat.title: Imagine Montessori School Valencia Overview
nodes_flat.topics: School type; Co-educational; Secular school; Montessori curriculum; British curriculum; UK education standards; School authorization; Integrated curriculum; Creative learning; Hands-on learning; Teaching staff; International teachers; Cultural perspective; Student development; Prepared environment; Student curiosity; Student autonomy; Creativity; Love of nature; Community belonging; Family guide; School organisation; School operation
nodes_flat.content:
Imagine Montessori School Valencia is a private, co-educational (mixed-gender education), and secular school that follows the Montessori curriculum at all levels, as well as the National Curriculum of England and Wales, adhering to the standards and guidelines of the UK Department of Education (DfES, Department for Education and Skills).

It is recognised and authorised by both the UK Department for Education (DfES) and the Spanish Ministry of Education.

Imagine has integrated Montessori pedagogy with the British national curriculum, taking a creative and hands-on approach to learning. This results in a wonderful combination that caters to the interests and needs of our students.

The teaching team consists of English-speaking teachers with international educational experiences, bringing a broad and enriching cultural perspective to the pedagogical content.

Our educational project is designed to support the development of our students at a psychological, physical, and emotional level, to nurture their minds. To achieve this goal, we offer a carefully prepared environment that promotes their innate curiosity and fosters their autonomy, creativity, love of nature, and sense of belonging to a community.

This handbook serves as a guide for families, providing information on our faculty, organisation, and the operation of our school.

nodes_flat.title: School Hours, Punctuality, Drop-off, Pick-up
nodes_flat.topics: School operating hours; Reception hours; Student arrival times; Student departure times; Punctuality importance; Late arrival impact; Early pick-up procedure; Early pick-up times; Absence notification; Schedule change reporting; Late arrival fees; Late pick-up fees
nodes_flat.content:
**School Opening Hours:** 8:00 a.m. - 5:30 p.m.
**Reception Hours:** 8:45 a.m. - 1:00 p.m. and 2:30 p.m. - 4:55 p.m.
**Arrival Time:** 8:45 a.m. - 9:00 a.m.
**Departure Time:** 4:40 p.m. - 4:55 p.m.

**The Importance of Punctuality**
We encourage families to arrive before 9:00 a.m., as:

*   Guides personally greet each student between 8:45 a.m. and 9:00 a.m., creating a welcoming atmosphere that encourages students to begin their activities proactively.
*   Arrivals after 9:00 a.m. disrupt the flow, as students who arrived on time have already started their activities, and guides are engaged in presenting materials. This may cause a late-arriving student to feel disoriented or unsettled.
*   Punctuality fosters a focused and productive environment, supporting students in developing autonomy and a sense of responsibility, while building positive habits for their future growth.

**EARLY PICK-UPS** –

For pick-ups, we ask that families or legal guardians arrive 5 minutes before the designated pick-up time.

As pick-ups outside the set schedule disrupt class dynamics and affect students' workflow, the only available pick-up times are as follows:

*   12:00 p.m.
*   2:30 p.m.
*   4:00 p.m.

Early pick-ups will take place at Reception.

**ABSENCES OR CHANGES IN ARRIVAL OR DEPARTURE TIMES** – Please notify in advance any student absences to:

*   By sending a Phidias message to the administration and the student’s Guide or tutor.
*   By calling the school from 8:45 am in cases of unforeseen circumstances.

Similarly, any changes in school arrival/departure times (e.g., medical appointments) should be reported, so they can be taken into account when planning daily activities and/or student logistics.

**LATE ARRIVALS** – Students who arrive late significantly disrupt the learning environment and the education of other students. When students arrive late, they miss essential instructions given at the beginning of the day, which can significantly impact their performance, regardless of their academic ability. Additionally, students may feel uncomfortable entering the classroom when others are already settled.

Late arrivals also interrupt the entire class and the teaching staff, compromising the learning experience for everyone.

Starting from the fifth late arrival during the school year, **a fee of €5 per late arrival will be charged on a monthly basis.** Information about late arrivals is recorded on a form; if a high frequency of late arrivals is detected, the Guides will arrange a meeting with the family and the teaching team to assess how we can support the family.

**LATE PICK-UPS** – If, for any reason, families arrive for pick-up after 4:55 pm, a "late pick-up fee" will be charged on the corresponding monthly invoice. The fees according to the delay are as follows:

*   From 4:55 pm to 5:15 pm – 5 € per late pick-up
*   From 5:16 pm to 5:30 pm – 10 € per late pick-up
*   From 5:31 pm to 5:45 pm – 20 € per late pick-up

nodes_flat.title: Rainy Day Student Logistics
nodes_flat.topics: Rain gear recommendation; Umbrella use discouraged; Rain pick-up notice
nodes_flat.content:
*   On rainy days, it is recommended that students **bring a raincoat and wellies**.
*   Additionally, **we do not recommend the use of umbrellas** when entering the school for safety reasons.
*   In the event of rain, you will **receive a notice** informing you about how the pick-up logistics will be handled.

nodes_flat.title: Student Arrival, Parking, Infant Community Entrance
nodes_flat.topics: Main entrance; Student/parent entry; Staff welcome; School parking; Parking restrictions; Health Centre parking fine; Infant Community entrance
nodes_flat.content:
**All students and parents/guardians** (except for the Infant Community) will enter the school through the door facing the school parking lot and walk to the main lobby, where staff will welcome the students and guide them to their classrooms.

All families should park their vehicles in the school parking lot to avoid traffic congestion and adhere to parking restrictions in the area. Please note that the parking area at the Health Centre on Calle Melissa is designated for its doctors, nurses, staff, and patients, and you may receive a fine if you park there.

Students from the **Infant Community** will use their own entrance on Calle Melissa and the roundabout.

nodes_flat.title: Student Pick-up by Age Group
nodes_flat.topics: Infant Community pick-up; Children's House pick-up; Primary Secondary pick-up; Pick-up order; Sibling pick-up policy; Sibling pick-up exceptions
nodes_flat.content:
*   **Infant Community Students**: They will be picked up at the Infant Community entrance on Calle Melisa.
*   **Children’s House Students**: They will be picked up in the Children’s House playground, located on Calle Melissa. They will be called one by one in the order of arrival.
*   **Primary and Secondary Students**: They will exit through the main door, facing the school parking lot. They will be called one by one in the order of arrival.
*   **Siblings**: All siblings will exit together through the door of the youngest child (unless different instructions are provided by the parents).

nodes_flat.title: Early Bird Afternoon Club Services
nodes_flat.topics: Early Bird service; Early Bird hours; Early Bird usage; Early Bird booking; Early Bird charges; Afternoon Club service; Afternoon Club hours; Afternoon Club activities; Afternoon Club purpose; Afternoon Club usage; Afternoon Club charges
nodes_flat.content:
Our **Early-Bird** service is available from **8:00 am to 8:45 am**. This service is available on all school days throughout the term and can be used either regularly or on a one-off basis. No prior notice or booking is required; attendance will be confirmed with the responsible person. The charge for this service will be included with the next monthly invoice and will not exceed the cost of the monthly service.

We are also introducing a new service to support families with work-life balance: the **Afternoon Club**, available from **4:40 pm to 6:00 pm**. This is a relaxed and creative space for children to stay at school after the regular day, engaging in playful and calm activities in a safe, familiar environment. This allows families who need extra time to pick up their children later, knowing they are well cared for.

Like Early-Bird, this service can be used either on a **regular basis** or **occasionally as needed**. Attendance will be recorded, and charges will appear on the monthly invoice. If the accumulated daily rate exceeds the monthly fee, the monthly rate will apply.

nodes_flat.title: School-Family Communication Channels
nodes_flat.topics: Communication importance; Communication platforms; Administrative language; Pedagogical language; Phidias overview; Phidias features; Phidias secondary; Transparent Classroom; One-way communication; Individual meetings; Progress reports; School newsletter; Daily communication; Tutorial hours; Administrative contact
nodes_flat.content:
At Imagine, we believe that smooth communication between families and the school is crucial. We aim for this communication to be swift and dynamic, as we know it contributes to the well-being of the students and the peace of mind of the families. To facilitate this, we use several communication platforms such as Phidias and Transparent Classroom.

The language of communication will be:

1) Administrative and secretarial matters: in Spanish and in English.
2) Pedagogical matters: in English with a link to the Spanish translation. The rationale for providing a link to the Spanish translation of pedagogical emails in English is to assist Spanish-speaking families in understanding the content, while allowing the teaching staff to dedicate the maximum amount of their time to activities related to the children rather than translating texts.

**Phidias** is our communication tool between our pedagogical team or administration and our families. Through Phidias, families have access to folders with relevant documents from the school, such as the monthly menu, photos, school calendar, yearbooks, and more. In each user’s personal space, you can access payments, the “pick-up authorisation” process, and the “Imagine Shop” for purchasing school materials.

For secondary students, Phidias will also be used for the grading and reporting system.

**Transparent Classroom** is a pedagogical communication tool used in the Infant Community, Children’s House, and Primary sections where our pedagogical team will share observations about the students and other weekly activities. It is a **one-way** communication channel from the teaching staff to the families.

The flow of communication from the school to families will also include the following channels:

**Individual Meetings with Families**: These meetings take place at the end of the first and third terms. The Guide provides a summary of the student's progress to the family, along with a written report, which will be sent via Transparent Classroom or Phidias, depending on the stage, before the meeting so that families can address any questions during the meeting.

**Newsletter**: The purpose of this periodic newsletter is to share with families the most relevant events in the school community.

**Daily Communication**: Guides in the Infant Community and Casa de Niños are available at drop-off and pick-up times to discuss anything families wish to share. For Primary and Secondary, communication with Guides will be via Phidias.

**Tutorials**: Each Guide has designated weekly tutorial hours to address specific issues throughout the year. You can request these directly from your Guide via Phidias.

For any administrative matters, you can contact our administration team directly through Phidias.

nodes_flat.title: Family-School Pedagogical Meetings
nodes_flat.topics: Family-school alignment; Educational guidelines; Quarterly meetings; Family-educator discussions; Pedagogical approach understanding; Recommended family attendance; Ask questions; Share experiences; Make suggestions; Student development involvement; Advance notice; In-person sessions; Online sessions; Work-life balance; Enriching in-person benefits; Group dynamics; Family interaction; Bilingual sessions; Family inclusion
nodes_flat.content:
We believe it is essential for the family and the school to be aligned on key educational guidelines. To facilitate this, we will invite families to quarterly meetings between families and educators, where various topics of interest will be discussed. These meetings are important for understanding the school’s pedagogical approach, so we recommend that at least one member from each family attends. They offer an opportunity for families to ask questions, share experiences, make suggestions, and become more involved in the development of our students.

We will provide sufficient advance notice so families can reserve these dates in their personal calendars.

To better accommodate families’ work-life balance, sessions will be offered both in person and online, making this resource accessible to as many families as possible. While the online option is very convenient, in-person sessions are more enriching due to the practical component that allows participation in group dynamics and provides a setting conducive to interaction among families.

Sessions will be conducted in both languages in separate sessions to ensure the inclusion of all families in this process.

nodes_flat.title: British Curriculum and Montessori Philosophy
nodes_flat.topics: British curriculum goals; Montessori philosophy; Student independence; Positive learning attitude; Accessible classroom materials; Child-sized furniture; Independent concept practice; Material presentation method; Guided material work; Independent material use; Practice and repetition; Independent skill acquisition; New material introduction; Work Cycle variety; Sensory materials; Hands-on learning; Concrete to abstract concepts; Qualified Montessori teachers; British curriculum environment; Staff professional development; DfES guidelines; British school in Spain; Montessori-UK curriculum alignment; Shared curricular goals; Reinforcing curiosity; Inquisitive thinkers; Considering others' opinions
nodes_flat.content:
Our primary goal is to provide a modern education that meets the objectives of the British curriculum while adhering to the methodology and philosophy of Maria Montessori. This approach fosters student independence and encourages a positive attitude towards learning. Our classrooms are organized and adapted to ensure that all materials are completely accessible to students, with furniture and shelves designed to their height, as well as work materials and accessories. All educational materials and the method of presentation are specifically designed to teach, demonstrate, and enable students to practice concepts independently. Once a student has been guided to work with a material and has received the appropriate presentation from the Montessori guide, they can continue to work with that material independently, as they already know how to use it. The practice and repetition of activities allow students to acquire skills and achieve goals independently. As guides introduce new materials, students have more variety to use during their Work Cycle, which will enhance their knowledge and develop/refine their skills.

Our materials are sensory and facilitate practical, hands-on learning, making the transition from concrete to abstract concepts smoother. Our teachers are qualified and experienced in using the Montessori method within a British curriculum environment. Additionally, we provide opportunities for the professional development of our staff.

As a British school in Spain, we follow the guidelines set by the Department for Education and Skills (DfES). The Montessori philosophy and the UK educational curriculum are aligned, teaching and emphasizing the same curricular goals, concepts, and skills. Both approaches aim to reinforce children's natural curiosity and desire to learn, helping them become inquisitive thinkers who include and consider the opinions of others.

nodes_flat.title: Early Years Foundation Stage Curriculum
nodes_flat.topics: Holistic education; Self-confidence; Emotional development; Personal development; Social development; Independent learning; Enjoyment; Infant Community focus; Core learning areas; PSED; Physical development; Communication; Language; Specific learning areas; Literacy; Mathematics; Understanding world; Expressive arts; Design; EYFS learning areas; Montessori Work Cycle; Integrated themes; Clubs; Yoga; Gardening; Sensory play; Crafts; Music; Drama; Storytelling; Individualized learning; Student pace; Mixed-age classrooms; Cooperation; Competition reduction; EYFS assessment; Montessori learning areas; Sensory area; Sensory activities; Senses refinement; Language preparation; Mathematics preparation; Classifying objects; Sorting objects; Environmental perception; Cultural area; Botany; Science; Geography; Zoology; History; Art; Hands-on experiences; Practical life; Daily tasks; Serving lunch; Cutting food; Dressing independently; Behavior guidelines; Courtesy; Social interaction; Language development; Auditory discrimination; Phonetic sounds; Sandpaper letters; Reading; Montessori reading materials; Grammar; Writing; Movable alphabet; Letter formation; Free composition; Circle Time; Show and Tell; Verbal communication; Self-expression; Mathematics; Sandpaper numbers; Number rods; Quantities; Addition; Subtraction; Abstract materials; Concrete materials; Number system; Montessori math materials; Rods; Beads; Spindles; Cubes; Counters; Place value; Visualization
nodes_flat.content:
The Early Years Foundation Stage (EYFS) and Primary Department’s primary goal is to provide students with a holistic education that aligns with our commitment to offering a school experience that fosters self-confidence, emotional, personal, and social development, and encourages independent learning and, most importantly, enjoyment.

In the Infant Community, the main focus is to ensure that students build a solid foundation within the three core areas of learning:

*   Personal, Social, and Emotional Development (PSED)
*   Physical Development (PD)
*   Communication and Language (C&L)

Once students demonstrate confidence and skill in these three areas, the guides begin to delve into the Specific Areas of Learning:

*   Literacy (L)
*   Mathematics (M)
*   Understanding the World (UW)
*   Expressive Arts and Design (EAD)

We address the seven EYFS learning areas within the Montessori Work Cycle, as well as through integrated themes and the creation of the following clubs: yoga, gardening, sensory play, crafts, music, drama, and storytelling.

We firmly believe that each student is an individual with unique strengths, potential, areas for development, interests, and learning styles. We work with each student at their own pace and allow them to engage with different concepts at a level that provides the right challenge for their age and abilities. In each classroom, students of three different ages interact, which promotes cooperation and reduces competition. Children are assessed within the seven EYFS Learning Areas, which align with the five Montessori learning areas.

Some examples of activities associated with each of the five Montessori areas are:

**SENSORY AREA**
Our sensory activities allow students to gradually refine each of their senses individually, which indirectly prepares them for later work in language and mathematics. As students develop their skills by classifying and sorting objects according to different characteristics, they enhance their ability to perceive and understand their environment.

**CULTURAL AREA**
We use a wide range of materials that allow students to gain knowledge and appreciation in subjects such as botany, music, science, geography, zoology, history, and art. Additionally, we plan activities that provide them with hands-on experiences.

**PRACTICAL LIFE**
By working on practical life skills, students learn to perform the tasks they see around them daily. For example, children serve their own lunch and also serve others. They are taught precise movements to help them use serving spoons, cut food, and dress independently. They are frequently given small guidelines on behavior and courtesy, teaching them how to navigate and interact with others in society.

**LANGUAGE**
The development of language skills begins with auditory discrimination games. Children are introduced to phonetic sounds using sandpaper letters. Once they have mastered these sounds, they start reading with Montessori materials, which gradually enhance and perfect their reading abilities.

Students also learn grammar and make progress in writing from the very beginning. This starts with writing on a board, using the movable alphabet, and progresses to writing on paper, copying texts, and free composition. Throughout this process, emphasis is placed on refining letter formation and positioning.

Additionally, language skills are reinforced during 'Circle Time' with activities such as 'Show and Tell,' which further supports their verbal communication and self-expression.

**MATHEMATICS**
We will use sandpaper numbers and become familiar with quantities using the We will use sandpaper numbers and become familiar with quantities using number rods. Operations such as addition and subtraction are covered using both abstract and concrete materials. All knowledge of the number system arises from the concrete manipulation of Montessori materials such as rods, beads, spindles, cubes, and counters. Tens, hundreds, and thousands are also incorporated using concrete materials, which help facilitate the visualization of the number system with these same Montessori materials.

nodes_flat.title: Primary (6-12) Curriculum Overview
nodes_flat.topics: Primary assessment standards; Montessori work cycle; National Curriculum subjects; Primary development stage; Conscious mind; Student interests; Idea, concept formation; Group work; Work organization; Social development; Mixed-age groups; Montessori guide supervision; Cooperative learning; Five Great Lessons; Great Lessons framework; Great Lessons topics; Curricular structure; Traditional subjects; Montessori curriculum content; Natural curiosity; Skill development; Material sequences; Abstraction; Paper work; Curriculum goals; STEM enhancement; Creative Lab workshop; Integrated projects; Hands-on learning
nodes_flat.content:
Students in Primary are assessed using the National Curriculum standards. The subjects of the National Curriculum continue to be covered within the Montessori work cycle.

Children between the ages of 6 and 12 enter a new stage of development. The child's absorbent mind evolves into a more conscious mind. Primary students show a keen interest in understanding reasons, explanations, and the concept of time. They begin to form their own ideas and concepts and are eager to explore these further.

Primary students are motivated to work in groups and start to organize themselves not only for companionship but also as a means of structuring their work. Our classrooms support social development through mixed-age groups, while Montessori guides observe and supervise the process carefully without interfering.

Aligned with an environment and teaching method that promotes cooperative work and exploration, the Montessori curriculum harnesses the natural sensitivity of this stage to energize Primary education. Each school year, students are introduced to five "Great Lessons" (as defined by Maria Montessori) through interpretation and specific materials such as timelines. These lessons provide a framework to which students add details, their perspectives, and understanding throughout their years in Primary. These stories include:

1.  History of the Universe
2.  History of Life on Earth
3.  History of Humanity
4.  Mathematics
5.  Language

These stories outline the curricular structure and encourage students to delve into traditional subjects, specifically those covered by the National Curriculum (such as science, history, geography, mathematics, and language and literature), with increasing depth each year.

Grammar, spelling, verb tenses, mathematics and its operations, and geometric constructions are all covered in the Montessori curriculum, benefiting from the students' natural curiosity and their constant desire to explore the world. These skills are strengthened with materials and exercises that continue from the sequences introduced in the Infant Community and Children’s House environments. The sequence of materials gradually leads students towards abstraction and paper-based work. The Montessori curriculum promotes deep understanding, creative thinking, problem-solving, teamwork, and skill refinement.

In addition to the traditional content covered by Montessori and aligned with the National Curriculum standards, we enhance STEM (Science, Technology, Engineering, and Mathematics) content through a cross-curricular workshop called “Creative Lab.” In this workshop, these disciplines are integrated into projects carried out by the students. These subjects play a crucial role in today’s society, and we equip students with the ability to interact with them in a hands-on manner.

nodes_flat.title: Psycho Pedagogical and Career Guidance
nodes_flat.topics: Psycho Pedagogical Guidance; Psychologist role; Special needs identification; External specialist advice; Specialist coordination; Teacher support; Daily routine adjustments; Collaborative action plans; School-home coordination; Individualized support; Support service fees; No direct diagnoses; No direct therapy; Vocational counseling; Academic, professional decisions; Educational, career options; Employability skills; CV writing; Interview preparation; Job search strategies; Decision-making guidance; Business, professional liaison; Career guidance experiences; Internship programs
nodes_flat.content:
**Psycho Pedagogical Guidance**

At Imagine, we believe that every student is unique and experiences different circumstances and/or specific conditions. This is why we consider the role of a psychologist to be essential in supporting students and their families, ensuring they gain the maximum benefit from their educational experience.

The Psycho Pedagogical Guidance Department is responsible for:

*   Identifying potential special educational needs in students.
*   Advising families on the type of external specialist they should consult for a diagnosis.
*   Coordinating with external specialists and supporting teachers in making the necessary adjustments to daily routines, as well as defining action plans to be implemented collaboratively at school and at home with strong coordination.
*   In certain cases, the school may determine that individualised support is required for the child’s benefit. If so, families will be informed about the type of support recommended (part-time/full-time, shared/individual) and the initial suggested duration. This additional service will be included in the monthly educational fees.

It is important to note that our department does not conduct diagnoses or therapeutic interventions directly; rather, it serves as a link and provides continuous support in the student’s educational journey.

**Career Guidance**

The Career Guidance Department is responsible for:

*   Vocational counselling: Helping students identify their interests, skills, and aspirations to make well-informed decisions about their academic and professional future.
*   Providing information on educational and career options: Offering insights into universities and career pathways.
*   Developing employability skills: Supporting students in CV writing, interview preparation, and job search strategies.
*   Guiding decision-making: Providing personalised advice so that students have the necessary information to choose the options best suited to their needs and goals.
*   Liaising with businesses and professionals: Facilitating career guidance experiences, including talks, company visits, and internship programmes.

nodes_flat.title: Encouraging Healthy Eating Habits
nodes_flat.topics: Student nutrition importance; Curricular links; High-quality ingredients; Varied diet exposure; Nutritionally balanced meals; Daily scratch cooking; Organic seasonal ingredients; Locally-sourced ingredients; No processed foods; No added fats sugars; No artificial additives; Low salt meals; Filtered water provision; No bottled water plastic; Medical needs adaptation; Variety of menus; On-site chef preparation; Nourishing students staff
nodes_flat.content:
Nutrition is a central pillar in student development, and its impact extends far beyond health and well-being. It has a direct connection with our body, as well as with the science and biology that provides natural curricular links. At Imagine Montessori School Valencia, we provide meals made with high-quality ingredients. Our goal is to expose our students to a variety of tastes, textures, and aromas that contribute to a nutritious and healthy diet.

Our meals and snacks are nutritionally balanced and are made daily from scratch using organic, seasonal, and locally-sourced ingredients whenever possible. We do not use processed foods with added fats or sugars, and all meals are free from artificial colourings and preservatives, and are low in salt. The water we use is filtered and available in the classrooms every day, reducing the need for bottled water and eliminating the use of plastics.

We adapt our dishes to medical needs certified by a specialist (gastroenterologist and allergist) and offer a variety of menus to accommodate the needs of our community. All our food is prepared daily on-site by our chefs at Imagine, with passion for nourishing the bodies and minds of our students and staff.

nodes_flat.title: Montessori food and waste education
nodes_flat.topics: Classroom meals; Mealtime help; Table setting; Food origin; Food preparation; Waste use; Garden care; Compost management; Recycling collection; Seasonal produce; Lunch incorporation; Gardening program; Montessori philosophy; Growing food; Food-land link; Seasonal cycles
nodes_flat.content:
Students eat together in their classrooms, and everyone helps before and after the meal. Students set the table daily using ceramic plates and metal cutlery.

We believe each student should have the opportunity to understand firsthand where their food comes from, how it is prepared, and how waste can be used productively. With this in mind, students take care of our gardens, manage our compost bin, and collect recyclable materials throughout the year. Depending on the season, vegetables, herbs, or fruits that students have planted, tended, and harvested are prepared and incorporated into the day's lunch.

Our gardening programme aligns closely with Montessori philosophy, offering real experiences with seeds, soil, water, and sunlight, as well as the life experience involved in growing food. As a result, students experience the process of where their food comes from and build a natural understanding of how food is linked to the land, its seasonal cycles, and the care required.

nodes_flat.title: Mealtime Logistics and Dietary Needs
nodes_flat.topics: Tooth brushing routine; Toothbrush/toothpaste requirement; Monthly menu sharing; Meal attendance notification; Occasional soft diets; Late arrival special diets; Late arrival menu guarantee; Afternoon snack recommendation
nodes_flat.content:
After meals, students brush their teeth. Each student should bring a toothbrush and toothpaste with their name on it on the first day of school.

We share the menus with families monthly through Phidias. As attendance is taken and sent to the kitchen at 09:30, it is important **to inform the administration before this time** of the following situations:

*   Students who need a soft diet occasionally.
*   Students who arrive late and have a special diet.

For students with adapted menus who arrive late without prior notice, we cannot guarantee menu adjustments for that day.

Although we offer a light snack at 16:00, we recommend that families provide an additional snack for pickup.

nodes_flat.title: Nap time provisions and costs

nodes_flat.content: After lunch, students in the Infant Community and Nursery (Casa de Niños) have a rest period. The school provides a fitted sheet for students who nap at the beginning of the year, which includes laundry service throughout the year (regardless of how frequently the service is used during the school year). You will be asked to provide a blanket when the weather requires it. The costs are detailed in the fee document for the relevant school year, available in the "Centre Documents" section on Phidias.

nodes_flat.title: Student Illness and Medication Policy
nodes_flat.topics: Do not attend school ill; Contagion risk; Isolate unwell students; Family collection unwell; Return after 24 hours; Fever attendance rule; Vomiting attendance rule; Diarrhea attendance rule; Strep throat return; Cold/flu attendance; Head lice attendance; Intestinal worms return; Rash attendance; Conjunctivitis return; Medication administration policy; Phidias authorization form; Medication drop-off; Home medication schedule; Oral medication only; Medical prescription required; Written family authorization; Keep student home; Treatment completion; Student ill at school; Family collection urgent; Emergency contact protocol; Call ambulance
nodes_flat.content:
It is essential that students do NOT attend school if they are ill or in the incubation period of an illness, as this poses a risk of contagion to both staff and classmates. When students are unwell and therefore unable to participate in class activities, they need to be isolated from the group while waiting for their family members to collect them.

In line with the recommendations of the Spanish Association of Paediatrics, students may only return to class after a **minimum of 24 hours** have passed since the last episode of fever, vomiting, or diarrhea. This allows time for recovery and helps prevent the spread of illness to teachers and classmates.

Below are some cases where a child should not be brought to school (this is not an exhaustive list):

*   Fever: If the child has had a fever, even if it has decreased after taking an antipyretic, or if they have had a fever in the last 24 hours (37.5ºC/99.5ºF or higher).
*   Vomiting: If the child has vomited in the last 24 hours.
*   Diarrhea: If the child has had diarrhea in the last 24 hours.
*   Streptococcal Pharyngitis: The child must be on antibiotic treatment for at least 24 hours before returning to school.
*   Cold or Flu: If the child has a runny nose and/or cough, especially if these symptoms have caused them to wake up during the night.
*   Head Lice or Nits: The child should stay home until treatment is completed.
*   Intestinal Worms: If the condition is not contagious, the child may return to school after the first cycle of treatment, provided a medical note confirms that the type of parasite is not contagious.
*   Rash: If the child has a rash.
*   Conjunctivitis: The child must be on antibiotic treatment for at least 24 hours before returning to school.

Imagine staff will not administer medications unless the Phidias tracking form specifically for this case is completed:

[How to authorize the administration of medication through Phidias](https://vimeo.com/1003109908/a6c73524dc)

Medications should not be given to students or placed in their bags but should be handed directly to the school office or the bus monitor, along with the properly completed and signed authorisation form.

If medication is required every 8 hours or more, families should adjust the schedule to administer it at home. For safety reasons and according to the guidelines from the Legal Service of the Ministry of Education, **our school will only administer oral medications, always with a medical prescription and written authorisation from the families.**

If a student shows symptoms of incubation or illness, or has head lice or intestinal parasites, parents will be asked to keep the student at home until the prescribed treatment is completed.

If a student becomes ill at school and the staff deems that they cannot participate in scheduled activities or may be contagious, the family will be contacted to collect the student **as soon as possible**. If we are unable to reach the family, we will contact the emergency person listed on the student's file.

If no contact can be located and an emergency arises, we will proceed to call an ambulance.

nodes_flat.title: Valencian Community Vaccination Adherence
nodes_flat.topics: Community health priority; Herd immunity principle; Vulnerable protection; Medical certificate requirement; Valencian vaccination schedule; Admission vaccination check; Four-year-old vaccination; Six-year-old vaccination; Twelve-year-old vaccination; Fourteen-year-old vaccination
nodes_flat.content:
The health of the student and our community is a priority. To achieve this, we adhere to the principle of "herd immunity" to protect the more vulnerable, including those who are immunocompromised. For this reason, families are requested to provide a medical certificate confirming adherence to the vaccination schedule of the Valencian Community at the following stages of the child:

1.  During the admission process to Imagine
2.  At 4 years old
3.  At 6 years old
4.  At 12 years old
5.  At 14 years old

nodes_flat.title: Student Behavior Incident Reporting
nodes_flat.topics: Behavior referral form; Incident reporting; Family notification; 24-hour notification; Three incidents meeting; Intervention meeting; Educational psychology team; Safe environment goal; Early intervention aim
nodes_flat.content:
If necessary, we have a referral form that will be completed by the teachers in the event of any incident related to a student's behavior that occurs during school hours. If a referral form is filled out, the families of the students involved in the incident will be informed by the teaching staff either in person or via email within 24 hours.

If three incidents occur, a meeting will be arranged with the family, the student's teacher, and the educational psychology team to assess how we can improve the situation and prevent it from happening again. At Imagine, our goal is to provide a happy and safe environment, and we strive to offer the best care to each student. Early intervention in behavioral incidents aims to foster a positive connection between the school and the home.

nodes_flat.title: Commitment to English Language Instruction
nodes_flat.topics: English instruction language; Family learning opportunity; Choosing English education; Encourage English reading; Support English speaking; Play English music; Watch English films; Demonstrate English interest
nodes_flat.content:
As a British school, our language of instruction is English. This presents an opportunity for many families to learn the language in which the students are taught.

By enrolling a student at Imagine Montessori School Valencia, you consciously choose to support their education in English.

This commitment includes:

*   Encourage reading in English
*   Support the student when they speak in English rather than Spanish
*   Play music in English as a fun way to improve listening skills, vocabulary, fluency, and pronunciation
*   If watching films and cartoons, it is recommended to do so in the original English version, if available
*   Demonstrate interest and commitment to the language by speaking in English and/or trying to learn it.

nodes_flat.title: Imagine School Dress Code Policy
nodes_flat.topics: No school uniforms; Optional school garments; Imagine Shop orders; PE uniform required; Excursion uniform; All clothing labelled; Student name label; Long label for hanging; Early years clothing; Comfortable practical clothing; Easy removal clothing; Early years shoes; Self-dressing shoes
nodes_flat.content:
**Applicable to all stages**

At Imagine, we believe that each student is unique, which is why we do not promote the use of school uniforms. However, we are also aware of the practicality that uniforms represent for some families, particularly in the early years, so we offer a variety of garments that you can view and order through Phidias in the Imagine Shop.

The only exception to the non-uniform policy will be for physical education classes and excursions, where all students will need to wear the Imagine sports shirt for safety and logistical reasons.

**ALL** clothing that enters the school must be labelled with the **student's name** (including socks, scarves, gloves, shoes, and blankets for those who nap, etc.) to make identification easier at pick-up time. Clothing that can be hung should have a sufficiently long label to allow the student to hang it up themselves.

**Early Years**
Daily clothing for the early years should be comfortable, practical, and easy to remove to encourage the student’s independence (elastic waistbands instead of belts, fasteners, or buttons). Shoes or slippers should be ones that the students can learn to put on and take off by themselves to facilitate their autonomy.

nodes_flat.title: Required Clothing and Equipment
nodes_flat.topics: Spare clothing requirements; Spare clothing 2-3 years; Spare clothing 4+ years; Replace used spare clothing; Closed footwear; Free play footwear; Sun hat; Sun protection; Morning sun protection; School sun protection; Self-application sun protection; Soft-soled shoes; Indoor slippers; Classroom/patio footwear
nodes_flat.content:
*   **Spare Clothing (Early Years and Primary):** Families must provide two complete sets of spare clothing (labelled) for students aged 2 and 3 years, and one complete set (labelled) for students aged 4 and above on the first day of school. These will be used if a student gets wet or dirty. If a set is used, it must be replaced with a clean one.
*   **Closed Footwear:** If students wear sandals or other minimal coverage footwear on hot days, please also provide a pair of closed-toe shoes or slippers to properly protect their feet during free play. They may change back into their sandals at the end of the day.
*   **Hat:** For the sunnier season: a hat that offers the maximum coverage possible (some versions cover up to the neck) to help prevent heatstroke and sunburn.
*   **Sun Protection:** Especially in summer and spring, sun protection is necessary. It is important to apply it in the mornings before coming to school, even on cloudy days. At Imagine, we will reapply it throughout the day. From the age of 3, it is recommended that they practice applying it themselves, encouraging their independence. Some brands offer a roll-on version, which can help with application.
*   **Soft-Soled Shoes:** Soft-soled shoes, or indoor slippers, are also required. These will be used both in the classroom and on the work patio throughout the school year.

nodes_flat.title: Nappy and Accident Policy
nodes_flat.topics: Nappy/wipe provision; Nappy supply notification; Nappy use location; Toilet training expectation; Accident support; Spare clothes use; Soiled clothing return; Spare clothes replenishment; Emergency clothes provision
nodes_flat.content:
For students who use nappies, families should provide **nappies and wipes**. When supplies run low, families will be notified to bring more. Nappies will only be used in the Early Years Community. The expectation is that all students will arrive at the Children's House toilet-trained.

For students who no longer use nappies but have occasional accidents, the centre staff will guide and support the student to clean themselves and change into the spare clothes provided by the family. Soiled clothing will be sent home at pick-up time. It will be necessary for the family to **provide a new set of spare clothes** to ensure that there are always two complete sets of spare clothes at school.

Families are requested to ensure that they have a change of clothes at school for any unforeseen circumstances.

nodes_flat.title: Birthday Celebrations Early Years, Children's House
nodes_flat.topics: Birthday celebration purpose; Family participation; Photo request; Student photo timeline; Student legacy; Classroom enduring gift; No food policy; Allergy prevention; School provides food; No party favours; No invitations; Primary, Secondary modifications; Age-appropriate celebrations
nodes_flat.content:
Birthday celebrations help students recognise the concept of time (how they have grown and changed since they were born). It also provides families with an opportunity to take part in an activity within the classroom.

We will contact families to request that you send the guide photos of the student from birth and a representative photo for each year up to their current age. The birthday celebration also includes an opportunity for the student, if they wish, to leave something behind that will endure for future classes, such as decorating a vase, bringing a book for the library, a candle, or some decoration for the classroom that will last over the years.

Due to potential allergic reactions, we have a no-foods policy for birthday celebrations. The school will provide lunch or a snack for the children as usual.

Similarly, we do not distribute party favours or invitations to students.

Primary and Secondary teachers may modify the type of celebration, as children grow and engage in more social celebrations with their peers.

nodes_flat.title: Children's Enrichment Programme and Enrollment
nodes_flat.topics: Enrichment Programme; Extracurricular activities; Children; Teenagers; Creativity; Social skills; Overall development; Meaningful experiences; Trained professionals; Online enrollment; Enrollment video
nodes_flat.content:
Our "Enrichment Programme" is an innovative extracurricular activities programme designed for children and teenagers, aimed at fostering their creativity, social skills, and overall development while they have fun and learn. Our approach focuses on providing meaningful and enriching experiences for participants, supported by a team of highly trained and enthusiastic professionals.

You can enrol your children through the online process provided for this purpose.

[How to enroll to our Enrichment Program](https://vimeo.com/990087464/7087fcf3d6)

nodes_flat.title: Returning Accidentally Taken School Materials
nodes_flat.topics: Child takes material; Return to reception; Return promptly; Part of set; Incomplete set unusable
nodes_flat.content: Occasionally, a child might accidentally take a piece of educational material from the school. In such cases, we kindly ask that you return the material to reception as soon as possible, as each small piece is part of a set that becomes unusable if incomplete.

nodes_flat.title: School Toy and Pacifier Policy
nodes_flat.topics: Toys prohibited; Comfort items naptime; Pacifiers naptime; Early Years pacifiers; Hygiene reasons; Language development
nodes_flat.content: Toys are not permitted at school, except for a comfort item for naptime if recommended by the guides. Similarly, for hygiene reasons and to aid language development, pacifiers are only allowed during naptime, if necessary, for children in Early Years.

nodes_flat.title: Student balls: use, storage, and school responsibility
nodes_flat.topics: Student ball allowance; Bus ball storage; Loose ball hazard; Lost ball responsibility; Outdoor use only; Indoor misuse confiscation
nodes_flat.content:
Students may bring balls to the centre. For students who use the bus, the ball must be kept in a bag, like other materials, and must not be loose, as this could cause an accident.

The school is not responsible for lost balls, including those that may end up in the ditch. It is important to communicate from home that balls should only be used in outdoor spaces, such as the playground, and if misused inside the premises, they will be confiscated.

nodes_flat.title: Lost and Found Item Recovery Policy
nodes_flat.topics: Lost and Found location; Monthly item display; Display notification; Unclaimed item clearing; Item donation; Item discarding; Labeling personal items
nodes_flat.content:
The Lost and Found will be located in one of the storage areas in the CH playground. To make it easier for families to recover misplaced items, once a month we will display all unclaimed items in the playground, usually on the last Friday of the month in the afternoon. We will inform you in advance of the exact day and time.

Please note that unclaimed items with no names will be cleared after this day and may be donated or discarded.

We strongly recommend labeling all personal belongings with your child’s name to ensure they can be easily returned.

nodes_flat.title: School Excursions: Participation and Consent
nodes_flat.topics: Excursion purpose; Scheduled, ad-hoc outings; Additional cost; Advance notification; Group participation; School transport; Start day at school; School pick-up; Bus transport rules; No external restraints; Consent form required; Phidias acceptance; Authorization guide; Non-participation attendance; Signed consent mandatory
nodes_flat.content:
During the school year, children will participate in excursions organised by the teaching team, which will complement various areas of learning within the curriculum. Some of these excursions will be scheduled in the school calendar, but it is possible that additional opportunities for outings may arise throughout the year, which the teaching team may consider relevant for the class and add to the schedule.

These excursions may incur an additional cost, which families will be informed of in advance.

Excursions are carried out in groups, which is an important part of the children's learning experience. Therefore, children must leave and return to school as a group, using the transport chosen by the school. This way, they can have the full experience of the activity, including the explanation of the dynamics in the Work Circle, reminders of behavioural rules, and the formation of groups or pairs for the bus, among other things. Consequently, participation in excursions is not permitted if the child does not start the day at school. Similarly, children will be picked up at the school at the usual times for each group.

If the outing involves taking a bus, transport company policies will not allow the use of additional seats, booster seats, or other external restraints.

To participate in any excursion, a specific consent form must be signed, which will be provided and can be accepted through Phidias without needing to go through the Secretary’s office.

[How to complete an Outing Authorization](https://vimeo.com/1002760877/fd5dbc823e)

For same-day excursions, students who do not participate may still attend school at the usual time.

**Children may participate in excursions ONLY if they have the signed consent form.**

nodes_flat.title: School Bus Routes and Policies
nodes_flat.topics: Bus routes; Service options; Route adjustments; Departure time; Arrival time; Service contracting; Late enrollment; Contract changes; Communication deadline; Absence reporting; Stop changes; Late pickup; Bus departure time
nodes_flat.content:
We offer three bus routes available throughout the academic year.

You can request an annual round-trip or one-way journeys.

Routes may vary from year to year based on families' needs. These routes are designed to accommodate as many families as possible, with consideration given to maintaining continuity of service for families who have used it in previous years.

This year we have adjusted the departure time by 10 minutes later to improve the experience of the last minutes of class. This change will not affect the arrival time at the stops, as the routes have been optimized for the 25/26 school year.

At the end of the academic year, we will provide a process for contracting the service. If you need the service once the school year has started, please contact [admin@imaginemontessori.es](mailto:admin@imaginemontessori.es). Availability will be subject to space.

Any change to the information provided in the contract must be communicated before 2:30 p.m. through the form that will be sent before the start of the school year. This form must be used if your child will not take the bus one day or if there is an exceptional change of stop. No changes will be accepted after that time or by phone or e-mail. If you need to pick up your child after 2:30 p.m., you can do so at the school by notifying the secretary between 2:30 p.m. and 4:30 p.m.. At 4:50 p.m. the bus will begin its route.

[How to edit your profile on Phidias](https://vimeo.com/1002778715/473248fe55?share=copy)

nodes_flat.title: Student Photos and Media Policy
nodes_flat.topics: Internal image sharing consent; External media authorization; Media consent impact; Annual yearbook photos; Professional photographer; Digital photo cost; Photo session notification; Digital photo provision; Sustainability
nodes_flat.content:
At Imagine, children work in shared spaces and often in teams. While working, their guides take photographs and videos to document their progress, with many of them featuring several children together. Therefore, it is part of our policy that all families at the centre give their consent to share images of their children with other families in the school.

Additionally, we occasionally share content in the media (press, our website, or social media). For this, a specific authorization will be required, which you should sign if you give your consent.

As we take this matter very seriously, it is possible that the total number of photos or videos of children whose images are not authorized for media use will be fewer than those with full permissions.

**Annual Yearbook:** During the school year, a professional photographer will take individual and group photos of all our children. We will provide digital copies to all families at an additional cost, which will be charged using the usual method. We will notify you in advance of the date on which the photo session will take place.

Given the variety of preferences among families regarding different formats for their various uses—photo albums (digital or physical), sharing with distant relatives and friends, multiple copies in different sizes, etc.—and following our sustainability principles to avoid printing photos that might not be used, we provide them digitally so that families can decide how to use them, either digitally or in print.

nodes_flat.title: Payment Policy and Fee Structure
nodes_flat.topics: Fees; Payment method; Annual fee revision; Direct debit; Monthly flat rate; Included costs; Additional services; Proforma schedule; Direct debit schedule; Questions contact; Schedule variations; Outings charge; Outing authorization; Outing refund; Enrichment charge; Enrichment timing; Enrollment fee; Enrollment refund; Refund conditions; Returned receipt fee; Bank commission
nodes_flat.content:
**Fees and payment method:** Imagine's fees, which can be revised annually, can be found updated on our website. Payment must be made monthly by direct debit.

**Frequency:** A flat rate will be charged monthly, which includes the prorated costs of the monthly fee, materials and registration for the following course. The bus service, morning classes, extracurricular activities, excursions, etc., will be charged monthly as appropriate for each family.

**Final receipts and proformas:** Proformas will be sent on the 25th of the previous month, and direct debits will be issued on the 1st of the month to which they correspond. There will be time between both dates to contact administration (Maria Luisa) and notify any questions. The specific days for issuing proformas and direct debits may vary to adjust to the work week.

**Outings:** Outings will be charged in the month in which they are carried out. The Outing Authorization processes in Phidias will always have a maximum response time. It may happen that it is after the invoices of the previous month have been issued. If a student cannot attend, as long as they inform us in a timely manner, and if it is possible to adjust the amount, the excursion will not be invoiced, and if it has already been invoiced, it will be refunded in the receipt of the following month.

**Enrichment:** The Enrichment charge will be quarterly, and will be issued at the beginning of the current quarter, except for the first, which will be from the last week of September/first of October, depending on the trial week and the particularities of each activity.

**Enrollment:** The enrollment fee for the following course, which is charged monthly from September to June, will be refunded in the event of non-continuity in the following course, as long as said non-continuity is communicated before February 1. In the event of a later communication, the charge will be maintained until June.

**Returned receipts:** In the event of a receipt being returned by the bank, the person responsible for payment will be responsible for the bank commission, which amounts to 2.5% of the total.

nodes_flat.title: Available Financial Aid Programs
nodes_flat.topics: Valencia City Council aid; Paterna City Council aid; Sodexo nursery vouchers; Working mothers tax deduction; Lunch aid exclusion; Childcare voucher exclusion; 0-3 schooling aid exclusion; Financial aid contact
nodes_flat.content:
Families at Imagine may apply for a range of government and private financial aids:

*   **Financial Aid Valencia City Council:** The Education Service of the Valencia City Council offers the School Voucher to families of children aged 0-6 years. Applications are available between May and April. The centre will provide information on deadlines and application methods when they become available.
*   **Financial Aid Paterna City Council:** The Education Service of the Paterna City Council offers the School Voucher to families of children aged 0-3 years. Applications are available in early May. The centre will provide information on deadlines and application methods when they become available.
*   **Sodexo Nursery Vouchers - Pluxe, UP Spain, Gourmet:** To assist our families, we also accept Sodexo nursery vouchers for Early Years Education.
*   **Tax Deduction for Working Mothers:** As government assistance, working mothers can deduct part of the schooling expenses with the Annual Income Declaration through form 233.
*   **Lunch Aid:** The Conselleria de Educación offers this aid, which is ONLY applicable to public and subsidised schools. This aid does not apply to a private school like Imagine.
*   **Childcare Voucher:** The Generalitat Valenciana offers this aid, which is ONLY applicable to public, subsidised, and private schools with a Spanish curriculum. This aid does not apply to a private school like Imagine, as we have a British curriculum from our Early Years Community.
*   **Financial Aid for 0-3 Year Schooling:** The Generalitat Valenciana offers this aid, which is ONLY applicable to public, subsidised, and private schools with a Spanish curriculum. This aid does not apply to a private school like Imagine, as we have a British curriculum from our Early Years Community.

If you have any questions regarding payments or financial aid, please contact the centre via [admin@imaginemontessori.es](mailto:admin@imaginemontessori.es).

children.title: Imagine Montessori School Valencia Overview
children.content:
Imagine Montessori School Valencia is a private, co-educational (mixed-gender education), and secular school that follows the Montessori curriculum at all levels, as well as the National Curriculum of England and Wales, adhering to the standards and guidelines of the UK Department of Education (DfES, Department for Education and Skills).

It is recognised and authorised by both the UK Department for Education (DfES) and the Spanish Ministry of Education.

Imagine has integrated Montessori pedagogy with the British national curriculum, taking a creative and hands-on approach to learning. This results in a wonderful combination that caters to the interests and needs of our students.

The teaching team consists of English-speaking teachers with international educational experiences, bringing a broad and enriching cultural perspective to the pedagogical content.

Our educational project is designed to support the development of our students at a psychological, physical, and emotional level, to nurture their minds. To achieve this goal, we offer a carefully prepared environment that promotes their innate curiosity and fosters their autonomy, creativity, love of nature, and sense of belonging to a community.

This handbook serves as a guide for families, providing information on our faculty, organisation, and the operation of our school.

children.title: School Hours, Punctuality, Drop-off, Pick-up
children.content:
**School Opening Hours:** 8:00 a.m. - 5:30 p.m.
**Reception Hours:** 8:45 a.m. - 1:00 p.m. and 2:30 p.m. - 4:55 p.m.
**Arrival Time:** 8:45 a.m. - 9:00 a.m.
**Departure Time:** 4:40 p.m. - 4:55 p.m.

**The Importance of Punctuality**
We encourage families to arrive before 9:00 a.m., as:

*   Guides personally greet each student between 8:45 a.m. and 9:00 a.m., creating a welcoming atmosphere that encourages students to begin their activities proactively.
*   Arrivals after 9:00 a.m. disrupt the flow, as students who arrived on time have already started their activities, and guides are engaged in presenting materials. This may cause a late-arriving student to feel disoriented or unsettled.
*   Punctuality fosters a focused and productive environment, supporting students in developing autonomy and a sense of responsibility, while building positive habits for their future growth.

**EARLY PICK-UPS** –

For pick-ups, we ask that families or legal guardians arrive 5 minutes before the designated pick-up time.

As pick-ups outside the set schedule disrupt class dynamics and affect students' workflow, the only available pick-up times are as follows:

*   12:00 p.m.
*   2:30 p.m.
*   4:00 p.m.

Early pick-ups will take place at Reception.

**ABSENCES OR CHANGES IN ARRIVAL OR DEPARTURE TIMES** – Please notify in advance any student absences to:

*   By sending a Phidias message to the administration and the student’s Guide or tutor.
*   By calling the school from 8:45 am in cases of unforeseen circumstances.

Similarly, any changes in school arrival/departure times (e.g., medical appointments) should be reported, so they can be taken into account when planning daily activities and/or student logistics.

**LATE ARRIVALS** – Students who arrive late significantly disrupt the learning environment and the education of other students. When students arrive late, they miss essential instructions given at the beginning of the day, which can significantly impact their performance, regardless of their academic ability. Additionally, students may feel uncomfortable entering the classroom when others are already settled.

Late arrivals also interrupt the entire class and the teaching staff, compromising the learning experience for everyone.

Starting from the fifth late arrival during the school year, **a fee of €5 per late arrival will be charged on a monthly basis.** Information about late arrivals is recorded on a form; if a high frequency of late arrivals is detected, the Guides will arrange a meeting with the family and the teaching team to assess how we can support the family.

**LATE PICK-UPS** – If, for any reason, families arrive for pick-up after 4:55 pm, a "late pick-up fee" will be charged on the corresponding monthly invoice. The fees according to the delay are as follows:

*   From 4:55 pm to 5:15 pm – 5 € per late pick-up
*   From 5:16 pm to 5:30 pm – 10 € per late pick-up
*   From 5:31 pm to 5:45 pm – 20 € per late pick-up

children.title: Student Arrival, Parking, Infant Community Entrance
children.content:
**All students and parents/guardians** (except for the Infant Community) will enter the school through the door facing the school parking lot and walk to the main lobby, where staff will welcome the students and guide them to their classrooms.

All families should park their vehicles in the school parking lot to avoid traffic congestion and adhere to parking restrictions in the area. Please note that the parking area at the Health Centre on Calle Melissa is designated for its doctors, nurses, staff, and patients, and you may receive a fine if you park there.

Students from the **Infant Community** will use their own entrance on Calle Melissa and the roundabout.

children.title: Student Pick-up by Age Group
children.content:
*   **Infant Community Students**: They will be picked up at the Infant Community entrance on Calle Melisa.
*   **Children’s House Students**: They will be picked up in the Children’s House playground, located on Calle Melissa. They will be called one by one in the order of arrival.
*   **Primary and Secondary Students**: They will exit through the main door, facing the school parking lot. They will be called one by one in the order of arrival.
*   **Siblings**: All siblings will exit together through the door of the youngest child (unless different instructions are provided by the parents).

children.title: Early Bird Afternoon Club Services
children.content:
Our **Early-Bird** service is available from **8:00 am to 8:45 am**. This service is available on all school days throughout the term and can be used either regularly or on a one-off basis. No prior notice or booking is required; attendance will be confirmed with the responsible person. We are also introducing a new service to support families with work-life balance: the **Afternoon Club**, available from **4:40 pm to 6:00 pm**. This is a relaxed and creative space for children to stay at school after the regular day, engaging in playful and calm activities in a safe, familiar environment. This allows families who need extra time to pick up their children later, knowing they are well cared for.

Like Early-Bird, this service can be used either on a **regular basis** or **occasionally as needed**. Attendance will be recorded, and charges will appear on the monthly invoice. If the accumulated daily rate exceeds the monthly fee, the monthly rate will apply.

children.title: School-Family Communication Channels
children.content:
At Imagine, we believe that smooth communication between families and the school is crucial. We aim for this communication to be swift and dynamic, as we know it contributes to the well-being of the students and the peace of mind of the families. To facilitate this, we use several communication platforms such as Phidias and Transparent Classroom.

The language of communication will be:

1) Administrative and secretarial matters: in Spanish and in English.
2) Pedagogical matters: in English with a link to the Spanish translation. The rationale for providing a link to the Spanish translation of pedagogical emails in English is to assist Spanish-speaking families in understanding the content, while allowing the teaching staff to dedicate the maximum amount of their time to activities related to the children rather than translating texts.

**Phidias** is our communication tool between our pedagogical team or administration and our families. Through Phidias, families have access to folders with relevant documents from the school, such as the monthly menu, photos, school calendar, yearbooks, and more. In each user’s personal space, you can access payments, the “pick-up authorisation” process, and the “Imagine Shop” for purchasing school materials.

For secondary students, Phidias will also be used for the grading and reporting system.

**Transparent Classroom** is a pedagogical communication tool used in the Infant Community, Children’s House, and Primary sections where our pedagogical team will share observations about the students and other weekly activities. It is a **one-way** communication channel from the teaching staff to the families.

The flow of communication from the school to families will also include the following channels:

**Individual Meetings with Families**: These meetings take place at the end of the first and third terms. The Guide provides a summary of the student's progress to the family, along with a written report, which will be sent via Transparent Classroom or Phidias, depending on the stage, before the meeting so that families can address any questions during the meeting.

**Newsletter**: The purpose of this periodic newsletter is to share with families the most relevant events in the school community.

**Daily Communication**: Guides in the Infant Community and Casa de Niños are available at drop-off and pick-up times to discuss anything families wish to share. For Primary and Secondary, communication with Guides will be via Phidias.

**Tutorials**: Each Guide has designated weekly tutorial hours to address specific issues throughout the year. You can request these directly from your Guide via Phidias.

For any administrative matters, you can contact our administration team directly through Phidias.

children.title: Family-School Pedagogical Meetings
children.content:
We believe it is essential for the family and the school to be aligned on key educational guidelines. To facilitate this, we will invite families to quarterly meetings between families and educators, where various topics of interest will be discussed. These meetings are important for understanding the school’s pedagogical approach, so we recommend that at least one member from each family attends. They offer an opportunity for families to ask questions, share experiences, make suggestions, and become more involved in the development of our students.

We will provide sufficient advance notice so families can reserve these dates in their personal calendars.

To better accommodate families’ work-life balance, sessions will be offered both in person and online, making this resource accessible to as many families as possible. While the online option is very convenient, in-person sessions are more enriching due to the practical component that allows participation in group dynamics and provides a setting conducive to interaction among families.

Sessions will be conducted in both languages in separate sessions to ensure the inclusion of all families in this process.

children.title: British Curriculum and Montessori Philosophy
children.content:
Our primary goal is to provide a modern education that meets the objectives of the British curriculum while adhering to the methodology and philosophy of Maria Montessori. This approach fosters student independence and encourages a positive attitude towards learning. Our classrooms are organized and adapted to ensure that all materials are completely accessible to students, with furniture and shelves designed to their height, as well as work materials and accessories. All educational materials and the method of presentation are specifically designed to teach, demonstrate, and enable students to practice concepts independently. Once a student has been guided to work with a material and has received the appropriate presentation from the Montessori guide, they can continue to work with that material independently, as they already know how to use it. The practice and repetition of activities allow students to acquire skills and achieve goals independently. As guides introduce new materials, students have more variety to use during their Work Cycle, which will enhance their knowledge and develop/refine their skills.

Our materials are sensory and facilitate practical, hands-on learning, making the transition from concrete to abstract concepts smoother. Our teachers are qualified and experienced in using the Montessori method within a British curriculum environment. Additionally, we provide opportunities for the professional development of our staff.

As a British school in Spain, we follow the guidelines set by the Department for Education and Skills (DfES). The Montessori philosophy and the UK educational curriculum are aligned, teaching and emphasizing the same curricular goals, concepts, and skills. Both approaches aim to reinforce children's natural curiosity and desire to learn, helping them become inquisitive thinkers who include and consider the opinions of others.

children.title: Early Years Foundation Stage Curriculum
children.content:
The Early Years Foundation Stage (EYFS) and Primary Department’s primary goal is to provide students with a holistic education that aligns with our commitment to offering a school experience that fosters self-confidence, emotional, personal, and social development, and encourages independent learning and, most importantly, enjoyment.

In the Infant Community, the main focus is to ensure that students build a solid foundation within the three core areas of learning:

*   Personal, Social, and Emotional Development (PSED)
*   Physical Development (PD)
*   Communication and Language (C&L)

Once students demonstrate confidence and skill in these three areas, the guides begin to delve into the Specific Areas of Learning:

*   Literacy (L)
*   Mathematics (M)
*   Understanding the World (UW)
*   Expressive Arts and Design (EAD)

We address the seven EYFS learning areas within the Montessori Work Cycle, as well as through integrated themes and the creation of the following clubs: yoga, gardening, sensory play, crafts, music, drama, and storytelling.

We firmly believe that each student is an individual with unique strengths, potential, areas for development, interests, and learning styles. We work with each student at their own pace and allow them to engage with different concepts at a level that provides the right challenge for their age and abilities. In each classroom, students of three different ages interact, which promotes cooperation and reduces competition. Children are assessed within the seven EYFS Learning Areas, which align with the five Montessori learning areas.

Some examples of activities associated with each of the five Montessori areas are:

**SENSORY AREA**
Our sensory activities allow students to gradually refine each of their senses individually, which indirectly prepares them for later work in language and mathematics. As students develop their skills by classifying and sorting objects according to different characteristics, they enhance their ability to perceive and understand their environment.

**CULTURAL AREA**
We use a wide range of materials that allow students to gain knowledge and appreciation in subjects such as botany, music, science, geography, zoology, history, and art. Additionally, we plan activities that provide them with hands-on experiences.

**PRACTICAL LIFE**
By working on practical life skills, students learn to perform the tasks they see around them daily. For example, children serve their own lunch and also serve others. They are taught precise movements to help them use serving spoons, cut food, and dress independently. They are frequently given small guidelines on behavior and courtesy, teaching them how to navigate and interact with others in society.

**LANGUAGE**
The development of language skills begins with auditory discrimination games. Children are introduced to phonetic sounds using sandpaper letters. Once they have mastered these sounds, they start reading with Montessori materials, which gradually enhance and perfect their reading abilities.

Students also learn grammar and make progress in writing from the very beginning. This starts with writing on a board, using the movable alphabet, and progresses to writing on paper, copying texts, and free composition. Throughout this process, emphasis is placed on refining letter formation and positioning.

Additionally, language skills are reinforced during 'Circle Time' with activities such as 'Show and Tell,' which further supports their verbal communication and self-expression.

**MATHEMATICS**
We will use sandpaper numbers and become familiar with quantities using the We will use sandpaper numbers and become familiar with quantities using number rods. Operations such as addition and subtraction are covered using both abstract and concrete materials. All knowledge of the number system arises from the concrete manipulation of Montessori materials such as rods, beads, spindles, cubes, and counters. Tens, hundreds, and thousands are also incorporated using concrete materials, which help facilitate the visualization of the number system with these same Montessori materials.
