children.title: Primary (6-12) Curriculum Overview
children.content:
Students in Primary are assessed using the National Curriculum standards. The subjects of the National Curriculum continue to be covered within the Montessori work cycle.

Children between the ages of 6 and 12 enter a new stage of development. The child's absorbent mind evolves into a more conscious mind. Primary students show a keen interest in understanding reasons, explanations, and the concept of time. They begin to form their own ideas and concepts and are eager to explore these further.

Primary students are motivated to work in groups and start to organize themselves not only for companionship but also as a means of structuring their work. Our classrooms support social development through mixed-age groups, while Montessori guides observe and supervise the process carefully without interfering.

Aligned with an environment and teaching method that promotes cooperative work and exploration, the Montessori curriculum harnesses the natural sensitivity of this stage to energize Primary education. Each school year, students are introduced to five "Great Lessons" (as defined by Maria Montessori) through interpretation and specific materials such as timelines. These lessons provide a framework to which students add details, their perspectives, and understanding throughout their years in Primary. These stories include:

1.  History of the Universe
2.  History of Life on Earth
3.  History of Humanity
4.  Mathematics
5.  Language

These stories outline the curricular structure and encourage students to delve into traditional subjects, specifically those covered by the National Curriculum (such as science, history, geography, mathematics, and language and literature), with increasing depth each year.

Grammar, spelling, verb tenses, mathematics and its operations, and geometric constructions are all covered in the Montessori curriculum, benefiting from the students' natural curiosity and their constant desire to explore the world. These skills are strengthened with materials and exercises that continue from the sequences introduced in the Infant Community and Children’s House environments. The sequence of materials gradually leads students towards abstraction and paper-based work. The Montessori curriculum promotes deep understanding, creative thinking, problem-solving, teamwork, and skill refinement.

In addition to the traditional content covered by Montessori and aligned with the National Curriculum standards, we enhance STEM (Science, Technology, Engineering, and Mathematics) content through a cross-curricular workshop called “Creative Lab.” In this workshop, these disciplines are integrated into projects carried out by the students. These subjects play a crucial role in today’s society, and we equip students with the ability to interact with them in a hands-on manner.

children.title: Psycho Pedagogical and Career Guidance
children.content:
**Psycho Pedagogical Guidance**

At Imagine, we believe that every student is unique and experiences different circumstances and/or specific conditions. This is why we consider the role of a psychologist to be essential in supporting students and their families, ensuring they gain the maximum benefit from their educational experience.

The Psycho Pedagogical Guidance Department is responsible for:

*   Identifying potential special educational needs in students.
*   Advising families on the type of external specialist they should consult for a diagnosis.
*   Coordinating with external specialists and supporting teachers in making the necessary adjustments to daily routines, as well as defining action plans to be implemented collaboratively at school and at home with strong coordination.
*   In certain cases, the school may determine that individualised support is required for the child’s benefit. If so, families will be informed about the type of support recommended (part-time/full-time, shared/individual) and the initial suggested duration. This additional service will be included in the monthly educational fees.

It is important to note that our department does not conduct diagnoses or therapeutic interventions directly; rather, it serves as a link and provides continuous support in the student’s educational journey.

**Career Guidance**

The Career Guidance Department is responsible for:

*   Vocational counselling: Helping students identify their interests, skills, and aspirations to make well-informed decisions about their academic and professional future.
*   Providing information on educational and career options: Offering insights into universities and career pathways.
*   Developing employability skills: Supporting students in CV writing, interview preparation, and job search strategies.
*   Guiding decision-making: Providing personalised advice so that students have the necessary information to choose the options best suited to their needs and goals.
*   Liaising with businesses and professionals: Facilitating career guidance experiences, including talks, company visits, and internship programmes.

children.title: Encouraging Healthy Eating Habits
children.content:
Nutrition is a central pillar in student development, and its impact extends far beyond health and well-being. It has a direct connection with our body, as well as with the science and biology that provides natural curricular links. At Imagine Montessori School Valencia, we provide meals made with high-quality ingredients. Our goal is to expose our students to a variety of tastes, textures, and aromas that contribute to a nutritious and healthy diet.

Our meals and snacks are nutritionally balanced and are made daily from scratch using organic, seasonal, and locally-sourced ingredients whenever possible. We do not use processed foods with added fats or sugars, and all meals are free from artificial colourings and preservatives, and are low in salt. The water we use is filtered and available in the classrooms every day, reducing the need for bottled water and eliminating the use of plastics.

We adapt our dishes to medical needs certified by a specialist (gastroenterologist and allergist) and offer a variety of menus to accommodate the needs of our community. All our food is prepared daily on-site by our chefs at Imagine, with passion for nourishing the bodies and minds of our students and staff.

children.title: Montessori food and waste education
children.content:
Students eat together in their classrooms, and everyone helps before and after the meal. Students set the table daily using ceramic plates and metal cutlery.

We believe each student should have the opportunity to understand firsthand where their food comes from, how it is prepared, and how waste can be used productively. With this in mind, students take care of our gardens, manage our compost bin, and collect recyclable materials throughout the year. Depending on the season, vegetables, herbs, or fruits that students have planted, tended, and harvested are prepared and incorporated into the day's lunch.

Our gardening programme aligns closely with Montessori philosophy, offering real experiences with seeds, soil, water, and sunlight, as well as the life experience involved in growing food. As a result, students experience the process of where their food comes from and build a natural understanding of how food is linked to the land, its seasonal cycles, and the care required.

children.title: Student Illness and Medication Policy
children.content:
It is essential that students do NOT attend school if they are ill or in the incubation period of an illness, as this poses a risk of contagion to both staff and classmates. When students are unwell and therefore unable to participate in class activities, they need to be isolated from the group while waiting for their family members to collect them.

In line with the recommendations of the Spanish Association of Paediatrics, students may only return to class after a **minimum of 24 hours** have passed since the last episode of fever, vomiting, or diarrhea. This allows time for recovery and helps prevent the spread of illness to teachers and classmates.

Below are some cases where a child should not be brought to school (this is not an exhaustive list):

*   Fever: If the child has had a fever, even if it has decreased after taking an antipyretic, or if they have had a fever in the last 24 hours (37.5ºC/99.5ºF or higher).
*   Vomiting: If the child has vomited in the last 24 hours.
*   Diarrhea: If the child has had diarrhea in the last 24 hours.
*   Streptococcal Pharyngitis: The child must be on antibiotic treatment for at least 24 hours before returning to school.
*   Cold or Flu: If the child has a runny nose and/or cough, especially if these symptoms have caused them to wake up during the night.
*   Head Lice or Nits: The child should stay home until treatment is completed.
*   Intestinal Worms: If the condition is not contagious, the child may return to school after the first cycle of treatment, provided a medical note confirms that the type of parasite is not contagious.
*   Rash: If the child has a rash.
*   Conjunctivitis: The child must be on antibiotic treatment for at least 24 hours before returning to school.

Imagine staff will not administer medications unless the Phidias tracking form specifically for this case is completed:

[How to authorize the administration of medication through Phidias](https://vimeo.com/1003109908/a6c73524dc)

Medications should not be given to students or placed in their bags but should be handed directly to the school office or the bus monitor, along with the properly completed and signed authorisation form.

If medication is required every 8 hours or more, families should adjust the schedule to administer it at home. For safety reasons and according to the guidelines from the Legal Service of the Ministry of Education, **our school will only administer oral medications, always with a medical prescription and written authorisation from the families.**

If a student shows symptoms of incubation or illness, or has head lice or intestinal parasites, parents will be asked to keep the student at home until the prescribed treatment is completed.

If a student becomes ill at school and the staff deems that they cannot participate in scheduled activities or may be contagious, the family will be contacted to collect the student **as soon as possible**. If we are unable to reach the family, we will contact the emergency person listed on the student's file.

If no contact can be located and an emergency arises, we will proceed to call an ambulance.

children.title: Student Behavior Incident Reporting
children.content:
If necessary, we have a referral form that will be completed by the teachers in the event of any incident related to a student's behavior that occurs during school hours. If a referral form is filled out, the families of the students involved in the incident will be informed by the teaching staff either in person or via email within 24 hours.

If three incidents occur, a meeting will be arranged with the family, the student's teacher, and the educational psychology team to assess how we can improve the situation and prevent it from happening again. At Imagine, our goal is to provide a happy and safe environment, and we strive to offer the best care to each student. Early intervention in behavioral incidents aims to foster a positive connection between the school and the home.

children.content:
As a British school, our language of instruction is English. This presents an opportunity for many families to learn the language in which the students are taught.

children.title: Imagine School Dress Code Policy
children.content:
**Applicable to all stages**

At Imagine, we believe that each student is unique, which is why we do not promote the use of school uniforms. However, we are also aware of the practicality that uniforms represent for some families, particularly in the early years, so we offer a variety of garments that you can view and order through Phidias in the Imagine Shop.

The only exception to the non-uniform policy will be for physical education classes and excursions, where all students will need to wear the Imagine sports shirt for safety and logistical reasons.

**ALL** clothing that enters the school must be labelled with the **student's name** (including socks, scarves, gloves, shoes, and blankets for those who nap, etc.) to make identification easier at pick-up time. Clothing that can be hung should have a sufficiently long label to allow the student to hang it up themselves.

**Early Years**
Daily clothing for the early years should be comfortable, practical, and easy to remove to encourage the student’s independence (elastic waistbands instead of belts, fasteners, or buttons). Shoes or slippers should be ones that the students can learn to put on and take off by themselves to facilitate their autonomy.

children.title: Required Clothing and Equipment
children.content:
*   **Spare Clothing (Early Years and Primary):** Families must provide two complete sets of spare clothing (labelled) for students aged 2 and 3 years, and one complete set (labelled) for students aged 4 and above on the first day of school. These will be used if a student gets wet or dirty. If a set is used, it must be replaced with a clean one.
*   **Closed Footwear:** If students wear sandals or other minimal coverage footwear on hot days, please also provide a pair of closed-toe shoes or slippers to properly protect their feet during free play. They may change back into their sandals at the end of the day.
*   **Hat:** For the sunnier season: a hat that offers the maximum coverage possible (some versions cover up to the neck) to help prevent heatstroke and sunburn.
*   **Sun Protection:** Especially in summer and spring, sun protection is necessary. It is important to apply it in the mornings before coming to school, even on cloudy days. At Imagine, we will reapply it throughout the day. From the age of 3, it is recommended that they practice applying it themselves, encouraging their independence. Some brands offer a roll-on version, which can help with application.
*   **Soft-Soled Shoes:** Soft-soled shoes, or indoor slippers, are also required. These will be used both in the classroom and on the work patio throughout the school year.

children.title: Nappy and Accident Policy
children.content:
For students who use nappies, families should provide **nappies and wipes**. When supplies run low, families will be notified to bring more. Nappies will only be used in the Early Years Community. The expectation is that all students will arrive at the Children's House toilet-trained.

For students who no longer use nappies but have occasional accidents, the centre staff will guide and support the student to clean themselves and change into the spare clothes provided by the family. Soiled clothing will be sent home at pick-up time. It will be necessary for the family to **provide a new set of spare clothes** to ensure that there are always two complete sets of spare clothes at school.

Families are requested to ensure that they have a change of clothes at school for any unforeseen circumstances.

children.title: Birthday Celebrations Early Years, Children's House
children.content:
Birthday celebrations help students recognise the concept of time (how they have grown and changed since they were born). It also provides families with an opportunity to take part in an activity within the classroom.

We will contact families to request that you send the guide photos of the student from birth and a representative photo for each year up to their current age. The birthday celebration also includes an opportunity for the student, if they wish, to leave something behind that will endure for future classes, such as decorating a vase, bringing a book for the library, a candle, or some decoration for the classroom that will last over the years.

Due to potential allergic reactions, we have a no-foods policy for birthday celebrations. The school will provide lunch or a snack for the children as usual.

Similarly, we do not distribute party favours or invitations to students.

Primary and Secondary teachers may modify the type of celebration, as children grow and engage in more social celebrations with their peers.

children.title: Children's Enrichment Programme and Enrollment
children.content:
Our "Enrichment Programme" is an innovative extracurricular activities programme designed for children and teenagers, aimed at fostering their creativity, social skills, and overall development while they have fun and learn. Our approach focuses on providing meaningful and enriching experiences for participants, supported by a team of highly trained and enthusiastic professionals.

You can enrol your children through the online process provided for this purpose.

children.content: Occasionally, a child might accidentally take a piece of educational material from the school. In such cases, we kindly ask that you return the material to reception as soon as possible, as each small piece is part of a set that becomes unusable if incomplete.

children.title: School Toy and Pacifier Policy
children.content: Toys are not permitted at school, except for a comfort item for naptime if recommended by the guides. Similarly, for hygiene reasons and to aid language development, pacifiers are only allowed during naptime, if necessary, for children in Early Years.

children.title: Student balls: use, storage, and school responsibility
children.content:
Students may bring balls to the centre. For students who use the bus, the ball must be kept in a bag, like other materials, and must not be loose, as this could cause an accident.

The school is not responsible for lost balls, including those that may end up in the ditch. It is important to communicate from home that balls should only be used in outdoor spaces, such as the playground, and if misused inside the premises, they will be confiscated.

children.title: Lost and Found Item Recovery Policy
children.content:
The Lost and Found will be located in one of the storage areas in the CH playground. To make it easier for families to recover misplaced items, once a month we will display all unclaimed items in the playground, usually on the last Friday of the month in the afternoon. We will inform you in advance of the exact day and time.

Please note that unclaimed items with no names will be cleared after this day and may be donated or discarded.

We strongly recommend labeling all personal belongings with your child’s name to ensure they can be easily returned.

children.title: School Excursions: Participation and Consent
children.content:
During the school year, children will participate in excursions organised by the teaching team, which will complement various areas of learning within the curriculum. Some of these excursions will be scheduled in the school calendar, but it is possible that additional opportunities for outings may arise throughout the year, which the teaching team may consider relevant for the class and add to the schedule.

These excursions may incur an additional cost, which families will be informed of in advance.

Excursions are carried out in groups, which is an important part of the children's learning experience. Therefore, children must leave and return to school as a group, using the transport chosen by the school. This way, they can have the full experience of the activity, including the explanation of the dynamics in the Work Circle, reminders of behavioural rules, and the formation of groups or pairs for the bus, among other things. Consequently, participation in excursions is not permitted if the child does not start the day at school. Similarly, children will be picked up at the school at the usual times for each group.

If the outing involves taking a bus, transport company policies will not allow the use of additional seats, booster seats, or other external restraints.

To participate in any excursion, a specific consent form must be signed, which will be provided and can be accepted through Phidias without needing to go through the Secretary’s office.

[How to complete an Outing Authorization](https://vimeo.com/1002760877/fd5dbc823e)

For same-day excursions, students who do not participate may still attend school at the usual time.

**Children may participate in excursions ONLY if they have the signed consent form.**

children.title: School Bus Routes and Policies
children.content:
We offer three bus routes available throughout the academic year.

You can request an annual round-trip or one-way journeys.

Routes may vary from year to year based on families' needs. These routes are designed to accommodate as many families as possible, with consideration given to maintaining continuity of service for families who have used it in previous years.

This year we have adjusted the departure time by 10 minutes later to improve the experience of the last minutes of class. This change will not affect the arrival time at the stops, as the routes have been optimized for the 25/26 school year.

At the end of the academic year, we will provide a process for contracting the service. If you need the service once the school year has started, please contact [admin@imaginemontessori.es](mailto:admin@imaginemontessori.es). Availability will be subject to space.

Any change to the information provided in the contract must be communicated before 2:30 p.m. through the form that will be sent before the start of the school year. This form must be used if your child will not take the bus one day or if there is an exceptional change of stop. No changes will be accepted after that time or by phone or e-mail. If you need to pick up your child after 2:30 p.m., you can do so at the school by notifying the secretary between 2:30 p.m. and 4:30 p.m.. At 4:50 p.m. the bus will begin its route.

children.title: Updating Contact and Family Information
children.content:
If your contact information or any other important records change during the school year, please update the new information in Phidias. It is important for the team to have the most current information available in case of an emergency.

[How to edit your profile on Phidias](https://vimeo.com/1002778715/473248fe55?share=copy)

On the other hand, if **your family situation** changes at home due to separation or divorce, we ask that you inform your guide as soon as possible so they can monitor any changes affecting your child.

children.title: Student Photos and Media Policy
children.content:
At Imagine, children work in shared spaces and often in teams. While working, their guides take photographs and videos to document their progress, with many of them featuring several children together. Therefore, it is part of our policy that all families at the centre give their consent to share images of their children with other families in the school.

Additionally, we occasionally share content in the media (press, our website, or social media). For this, a specific authorization will be required, which you should sign if you give your consent.

As we take this matter very seriously, it is possible that the total number of photos or videos of children whose images are not authorized for media use will be fewer than those with full permissions.

**Annual Yearbook:** During the school year, a professional photographer will take individual and group photos of all our children. We will provide digital copies to all families at an additional cost, which will be charged using the usual method. We will notify you in advance of the date on which the photo session will take place.

Given the variety of preferences among families regarding different formats for their various uses—photo albums (digital or physical), sharing with distant relatives and friends, multiple copies in different sizes, etc.—and following our sustainability principles to avoid printing photos that might not be used, we provide them digitally so that families can decide how to use them, either digitally or in print.

children.title: Payment Policy and Fee Structure
children.content:
**Fees and payment method:** Imagine's fees, which can be revised annually, can be found updated on our website. Payment must be made monthly by direct debit.

**Frequency:** A flat rate will be charged monthly, which includes the prorated costs of the monthly fee, materials and registration for the following course. The bus service, morning classes, extracurricular activities, excursions, etc., will be charged monthly as appropriate for each family.

**Final receipts and proformas:** Proformas will be sent on the 25th of the previous month, and direct debits will be issued on the 1st of the month to which they correspond. There will be time between both dates to contact administration (Maria Luisa) and notify any questions. The specific days for issuing proformas and direct debits may vary to adjust to the work week.

**Outings:** Outings will be charged in the month in which they are carried out. The Outing Authorization processes in Phidias will always have a maximum response time. It may happen that it is after the invoices of the previous month have been issued. If a student cannot attend, as long as they inform us in a timely manner, and if it is possible to adjust the amount, the excursion will not be invoiced, and if it has already been invoiced, it will be refunded in the receipt of the following month.

**Enrichment:** The Enrichment charge will be quarterly, and will be issued at the beginning of the current quarter, except for the first, which will be from the last week of September/first of October, depending on the trial week and the particularities of each activity.

**Enrollment:** The enrollment fee for the following course, which is charged monthly from September to June, will be refunded in the event of non-continuity in the following course, as long as said non-continuity is communicated before February 1. In the event of a later communication, the charge will be maintained until June.

**Returned receipts:** In the event of a receipt being returned by the bank, the person responsible for payment will be responsible for the bank commission, which amounts to 2.5% of the total.

children.title: Available Financial Aid Programs
children.content:
Families at Imagine may apply for a range of government and private financial aids:

*   **Financial Aid Valencia City Council:** The Education Service of the Valencia City Council offers the School Voucher to families of children aged 0-6 years. Applications are available between May and April. The centre will provide information on deadlines and application methods when they become available.
*   **Financial Aid Paterna City Council:** The Education Service of the Paterna City Council offers the School Voucher to families of children aged 0-3 years. Applications are available in early May. The centre will provide information on deadlines and application methods when they become available.
*   **Sodexo Nursery Vouchers - Pluxe, UP Spain, Gourmet:** To assist our families, we also accept Sodexo nursery vouchers for Early Years Education.
*   **Tax Deduction for Working Mothers:** As government assistance, working mothers can deduct part of the schooling expenses with the Annual Income Declaration through form 233.
*   **Lunch Aid:** The Conselleria de Educación offers this aid, which is ONLY applicable to public and subsidised schools. This aid does not apply to a private school like Imagine.
*   **Childcare Voucher:** The Generalitat Valenciana offers this aid, which is ONLY applicable to public, subsidised, and private schools with a Spanish curriculum. This aid does not apply to a private school like Imagine, as we have a British curriculum from our Early Years Community.
*   **Financial Aid for 0-3 Year Schooling:** The Generalitat Valenciana offers this aid, which is ONLY applicable to public, subsidised, and private schools with a Spanish curriculum. This aid does not apply to a private school like Imagine, as we have a British curriculum from our Early Years Community.

If you have any questions regarding payments or financial aid, please contact the centre via [admin@imaginemontessori.es](mailto:admin@imaginemontessori.es).
